summary: Ronacher argues that parts of Python’s standard library are poorly designed and can hide severe performance traps. He recounts a real-world issue in Werkzeug where serving static files was capped at 50 requests/second. The bottleneck was `mimetypes.guess_type`, which unexpectedly reloaded its database on every call due to how it monkey-patches itself during `init()`. Because he imported `guess_type` directly, the monkey-patch never replaced the local reference, causing repeated initialization. He concludes that the stdlib behavior is flawed enough to warrant a bug ticket and urges others to change their imports.
key_points: The stdlib contains modules with design flaws and inconsistent quality.
A single function call (`mimetypes.guess_type`) caused a 10x slowdown in request throughput.
The function’s implementation relies on runtime monkey-patching after initialization.
Using `from mimetypes import guess_type` bypasses the monkey-patch and triggers repeated database loads.
The fix is to import the module and call `mimetypes.guess_type` to benefit from the patched function.
money_quotes: I hate the stdlib. There! I said it.
I noticed that Werkzeug's SharedDataMiddleware was just delivering 50 requests a second.
Until I removed the call to mimetypes.guess_type.
WTF? And then it hit me.
So on every call the database was reloaded.
If yes, change it to the following import: import mimetypes.
And now I file a ticket in the Python bug tracker.
key_insight: A subtle stdlib design quirk—monkey-patching after init—can turn a harmless import style into a massive performance regression.
